original_hypothesis.title: setDistributableERC20s Callable During Active Distribution Causes Permanent Contract Lock
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: setDistributableERC20s has no check for LockedForDistribution. If called during an active distribution, it changes the distributableERC20s array while erc20EntitlementPerUnit was sized for the old array. In distribute(), the inner loop iterates over `distributableERC20s.length` (new length) and accesses `erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j]` (old length). If the new array is longer, this causes an array out-of-bounds revert. Since LockedForDistribution is true and can only be cleared by completing the distribution (which now always reverts), the contract is permanently locked — no transfers, mints, or burns can ever occur again.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The missing LockedForDistribution guard on setDistributableERC20s is a code defect. While it requires admin action, admins may use governance tools or automated scripts that don't coordinate with distribution state. The permanent lock impact makes this critical.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setDistributableERC20s has onlyOwner but no LockedForDistribution check; distribute() at lines 167-175 iterates distributableERC20s.length and indexes erc20EntitlementPerUnit

preconditions: Owner calls setDistributableERC20s while LockedForDistribution is true
New distributableERC20s array length differs from erc20EntitlementPerUnit length

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Array length mismatch: distributableERC20s.length (modified) != erc20EntitlementPerUnit.length (frozen at _beginDistribution)

analysis_questions: Can the admin accidentally trigger this via governance or script?
Is there any way to recover from the permanent lock?
What if the new array is shorter — does it silently distribute wrong tokens?
validation_requirements: Start distribution, call setDistributableERC20s with longer array, call distribute, verify OOB revert
Verify contract is permanently locked after this sequence
Test with shorter array to check silent misallocation

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Distribution starts with 1 distributable ERC20 (erc20EntitlementPerUnit.length=1). Admin calls setDistributableERC20s with 3 tokens. Next distribute() call iterates j=0,1,2 but erc20EntitlementPerUnit only has index 0. Access to erc20EntitlementPerUnit[1] causes array OOB revert. Distribution can never complete. Contract permanently locked.
prerequisites: Admin calls setDistributableERC20s during active distribution

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All ERC20 tokens permanently frozen (no transfers, mints, burns); all reward tokens locked
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders and the protocol itself

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for setDistributableERC20s during active distribution

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by checking LockedForDistribution state before admin calls

validation_result.mechanism: Array length mismatch via missing distribution lock guard
validation_result.impact_summary: setDistributableERC20s can be called during active distribution, causing array out-of-bounds revert in distribute() and locking the contract. Recovery is possible by the owner calling setDistributableERC20s again, so 'permanent' is slightly overstated, but the code defect is real.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies that setDistributableERC20s has no check for LockedForDistribution. During an active distribution, _beginDistribution() populates erc20EntitlementPerUnit to match distributableERC20s.length at that moment. If the owner then calls setDistributableERC20s with a longer array, the distribute() inner loop iterates over the new distributableERC20s.length but accesses erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j], which is still sized to the old length. In Solidity 0.8.12, accessing an array out of bounds causes a panic revert. Since LockedForDistribution is true and _endDistribution can only be called when all holders are processed (which requires distribute to complete without revert), the contract enters a deadlocked state. However, I note the owner CAN recover by calling setDistributableERC20s again with an array of compatible length, since that function also lacks the lock check. This makes it recoverable but still a critical defect.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct evidence found in source code. setDistributableERC20s (lines 291-296) has only onlyOwner guard. distribute() inner loop (lines 175-182) iterates distributableERC20s.length and indexes erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j]. _beginDistribution() (lines 207-228) sizes erc20EntitlementPerUnit to match distributableERC20s at call time. No synchronization between these arrays exists after _beginDistribution.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Missing require(!LockedForDistribution) guard allows the owner to change distributableERC20s during an active distribution, breaking the invariant that distributableERC20s.length == erc20EntitlementPerUnit.length while LockedForDistribution is true.

Loop iterates over distributableERC20s.length (which may have been modified) but indexes erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j] (which was sized at _beginDistribution). If distributableERC20s is now longer, erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j] causes an array out-of-bounds panic revert.
erc20EntitlementPerUnit is sized to match distributableERC20s.length at this moment. If distributableERC20s is later changed, the lengths diverge.

calling_sequence: 1. distribute(N) is called, LockedForDistribution is false → calls _beginDistribution()
2. _beginDistribution() sets LockedForDistribution=true, populates erc20EntitlementPerUnit with length L (matching distributableERC20s.length at that moment)
3. distribute() processes some holders (partial batch), nextDistributionRecipient < holders.length, distribution remains locked
4. Owner calls setDistributableERC20s() with array of length L' > L (allowed because no LockedForDistribution check)
5. distribute(M) is called again, LockedForDistribution is true so _beginDistribution is skipped
6. Inner loop iterates j from 0 to L'-1, when j >= L, erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j] triggers Solidity 0.8.x array OOB panic
7. distribute() always reverts, _endDistribution() is never reached, LockedForDistribution remains true
8. All transfers, mints, burns revert due to require(!LockedForDistribution) in _beforeTokenTransfer
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: distribute() → inner loop at line 175 iterates distributableERC20s.length → erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j] at line 177 reverts when j >= old array length. The only exit from locked state is _endDistribution() at line 190, which requires nextDistributionRecipient == holders.length, but distribute always reverts before updating nextDistributionRecipient. Note: owner CAN call setDistributableERC20s again to fix the array length, providing a recovery path.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this vulnerability. The deployment script sets MinDistributionPeriod to 10 and distributableERC20s to [erc20TestAddressA], but the vulnerability is triggered by runtime admin behavior, not deployment parameters.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is valid: (1) Distribution starts with N distributable ERC20s, sizing erc20EntitlementPerUnit to N. (2) Owner calls setDistributableERC20s with M > N tokens during the active distribution. (3) Next distribute() call hits OOB at erc20EntitlementPerUnit[N]. (4) Contract is locked. The scenario requires admin action during distribution, which could happen via governance scripts, multisig proposals executed without coordination, or simple operational error. The 'permanent' lock claim is slightly overstated because the owner could call setDistributableERC20s again to restore a compatible array length, but if the owner doesn't realize this recovery path, the contract remains locked.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity code defect. If triggered with a longer array, all transfers/mints/burns are blocked until the owner recognizes and fixes the issue by resetting distributableERC20s. If triggered with a shorter array (no revert), entitlements calculated for old tokens are applied to new tokens at wrong indices, causing misallocation of funds. Both scenarios represent real harm from a missing guard.
recommended_mitigations: Add `require(!LockedForDistribution, "cannot change distributable ERC20s during distribution")` to setDistributableERC20s
Consider making erc20EntitlementPerUnit a snapshot that is independent of later changes to distributableERC20s by also snapshotting the token addresses at _beginDistribution
Add a general emergency unlock mechanism or admin override for recovery from unexpected lock states
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LiquidInfrastructureERC20 with 1 distributable ERC20 and MinDistributionPeriod=1. 2. Mint tokens to 2+ approved holders. 3. Fund contract with distributable ERC20. 4. Advance blocks past MinDistributionPeriod. 5. Call distribute(1) to start distribution and process 1 holder. 6. As owner, call setDistributableERC20s with 3 token addresses. 7. Call distribute(1) again → expect OOB revert. 8. Attempt transfer → expect revert due to LockedForDistribution. 9. Verify recovery: call setDistributableERC20s with 1 token, then distribute completes.
uncertainties: The 'permanent lock' claim is overstated — the owner can recover by calling setDistributableERC20s again with a compatible-length array. But this requires the owner to understand the recovery mechanism.
If the new array is shorter, no revert occurs but entitlements are misallocated (wrong token amounts for wrong tokens), which is a separate silent fund misallocation issue.
additional_testing_needed: Test with shorter new array during distribution to verify silent misallocation behavior
Test recovery path: setDistributableERC20s back to original length during locked state, then complete distribution
Test with same length but different token addresses to verify wrong-token distribution
